posts.title: Agents that work vs agents that demo
posts.hook: The gap between AI agent demos and AI agents in production is about 6 months of error handling.
posts.body:
The gap between AI agent demos and AI agents in production is about 6 months of error handling.

I've been building agentic workflows for GTM since last year. The pattern I keep seeing:

Week 1: Agent works perfectly on 10 test cases. You feel like a genius.
Week 3: Agent hits edge case #47. You add a retry loop.
Week 8: You realize 30% of failures are silent. No errors, just wrong outputs that look right.
Week 16: You've built more observability tooling than actual agent logic.

The frameworks get all the attention. LangChain, CrewAI, whatever ships next Tuesday. But the teams actually running agents in production aren't talking about frameworks. They're talking about evaluation harnesses, fallback chains, and how to detect when an agent confidently does the wrong thing.

My enrichment pipeline runs ~2,000 records a night through Claude. The agent code is maybe 200 lines. The validation, retry, and monitoring code around it is 1,400 lines.

That ratio tells you everything about where we actually are with agents.

The unlock isn't better orchestration. It's better failure modes. Agents that know when they don't know. Agents that fail loud instead of failing confident.

If your agent doesn't have a way to say "I'm not sure about this one," it's a demo, not a system.
posts.cta: What's your agent failure-to-feature code ratio looking like?

Shares a case study on automating data pipelines with agents, reducing manual dev time by 70%. Discusses infrastructure bottlenecks in production deployments.

remixes.label: iceberg-architecture
remixes.hook: Every AI agent demo you've seen is the tip of the iceberg. The 90% underneath is logging, retries, and fallback chains nobody talks about.
remixes.body:
Every AI agent demo you've seen is the tip of the iceberg. The 90% underneath is logging, retries, and fallback chains nobody talks about.

I shipped my first agentic GTM workflow last year. The core logic took a weekend. Making it production-ready took five months.

Here's what the demo didn't need but production demanded:

A dead letter queue for when the LLM returns valid JSON that's semantically wrong. A shadow pipeline that runs the same input through a cheaper model and diffs the outputs. Alert thresholds tuned to catch drift before your sales team notices the lead scores are off.

The unsexy truth about AI agents in production: most of your code isn't the agent. It's the immune system around the agent.

I now spend roughly 70% of dev time on observability and error recovery. 20% on the actual prompt engineering. 10% on the integration glue everyone thinks is the hard part.

The teams winning with agents aren't the ones with the best prompts. They're the ones who built the best monitoring. Because every agent degrades silently until you instrument it not to.
remixes.cta: What's the ratio of agent logic to error handling in your production systems?

remixes.label: silent-failure-autopsy
remixes.hook: The scariest thing about AI agents isn't when they fail loudly. It's when they succeed incorrectly and nobody notices for three weeks.
remixes.body:
The scariest thing about AI agents isn't when they fail loudly. It's when they succeed incorrectly and nobody notices for three weeks.

I caught this in my own system. An enrichment agent was returning plausible but fabricated company data. Valid JSON. Reasonable-looking revenue numbers. Just wrong. It passed every structural check I had.

The only reason I found it was a client mentioned a data point that didn't match their own records.

That incident changed how I build agents entirely. Now every agent output gets a confidence hash. Not a score the LLM assigns itself, because those are meaningless. A structural fingerprint based on source attribution, response consistency across runs, and cross-reference against known data.

It added two weeks to the build. It's caught four silent failures since.

The agent reliability problem isn't about retries or better prompts. It's about accepting that your agent will be confidently wrong on a regular basis and designing for that assumption from day one.

Most agent architectures optimize for the happy path. Production agent architectures optimize for detecting the unhappy path before it reaches a human.
remixes.cta: Have you caught an agent being confidently wrong? How did you find it?

If you're deploying AI agents beyond demos, here's the framework I recommend.

First, accept that the core agent logic is maybe 20% of your production codebase. The rest is infrastructure: retry policies, dead letter handling, output validation, and drift monitoring.

I've been building agentic GTM workflows for over a year now. The pattern is consistent across every deployment.

Week 1: Agent works on your test cases. Everything looks clean.
Week 3: Edge cases start surfacing. You add retry loops and input sanitization.
Week 8: You discover that 30% of failures are silent. No errors thrown, just wrong outputs that look right.

My recommendation: before you write a single line of agent logic, design your observability layer. Instrument output validation. Build comparison pipelines that catch semantic drift. Set up alerts on output distribution changes, not just error rates.

The teams I see succeeding treat their agents like any other critical production system. They don't trust them. They verify continuously. And they budget 3x the development time for hardening versus the initial build.

Start with the monitoring. The clever prompts can come later.
tones.provocateur:
Your agent demo is a lie and you know it.

That slick 30-second clip of your AI agent booking meetings, enriching leads, writing sequences? It works on 10 hand-picked inputs in a controlled environment. Congratulations. You built a very expensive macro.

I've been shipping agentic GTM workflows since last year. Here's what actually happens in production.

Week 1: Works perfectly. You post the demo on LinkedIn.
Week 3: Edge case #47 breaks it. You quietly add a retry loop.
Week 8: You realize a third of your failures aren't even failures. They're the agent returning plausible garbage that passes every check.

The dirty secret of the AI agent space right now: most "production" agents have a human checking every output behind the scenes. The automation is theater.

Real production agents are ugly. They're 80% error handling, 15% logging, and 5% the actual prompt everyone wants to talk about. Nobody posts screenshots of their dead letter queue or their output diff pipeline.

If your agent doesn't have better observability than your web app, it's not production-ready. It's a demo with a cron job.

posts.title: Revenue ops is just infrastructure engineering
posts.hook: Hot take: the best revenue ops people I know would be called platform engineers at a tech company.
posts.body:
Hot take: the best revenue ops people I know would be called platform engineers at a tech company.

They're not "configuring Salesforce." They're designing data pipelines that route leads through scoring models, enrichment layers, and routing logic before a human ever sees them.

The tool names are different. HubSpot instead of Kafka. Clay instead of dbt. Attio instead of Postgres. But the work is the same... build reliable systems that move data from point A to point B without losing fidelity.

I watched a rev ops lead debug a broken lead routing flow last week. She was tracing data through 4 systems, checking field mappings at each handoff, validating that a webhook fired correctly. That's incident response. That's infrastructure debugging.

The gap in most GTM orgs isn't strategy. It's engineering rigor applied to their revenue systems.

No one version-controls their HubSpot workflows. No one writes tests for their lead scoring model. No one has a staging environment for their CRM.

And then they wonder why things break every quarter when someone changes a dropdown value.

The teams winning right now treat their GTM stack like software. Because it is software. It's just software that sales leadership doesn't know is software.
posts.cta: Anyone else treating their CRM like a codebase? What does your version control look like?

inspired_by.author: Briana McNally
inspired_by.summary: Explores the evolution of sales ops into revenue operations, highlighting tools for cross-functional alignment. Shares playbook for ops teams to drive efficiency in forecasting and deal velocity. Stresses the need for ops leaders to think like engineers.

remixes.label: crm-as-codebase
remixes.hook: I version control my CRM the same way I version control my code. And yes, I mean git commits for HubSpot workflow changes.
remixes.body:
I version control my CRM the same way I version control my code. And yes, I mean git commits for HubSpot workflow changes.

Sounds extreme. But think about what a mature revenue ops setup actually looks like. You have data pipelines ingesting from multiple sources. Transformation logic that scores and enriches records. Routing rules that determine which human sees what, and when. Reporting layers that aggregate everything into dashboards.

That's not CRM configuration. That's infrastructure engineering with a drag-and-drop UI.

The problem is that most companies treat their CRM like a settings page. Change something, hope it works, no record of what changed or why. Then six months later, nobody knows why leads from one channel skip the scoring model entirely.

I started exporting my CRM logic as code. Every workflow, every scoring rule, every routing decision lives in a repo. When something breaks, I can diff the change. When I onboard someone, they can read the logic like documentation.

The revenue ops people who think in systems, who see data flows instead of dropdown menus, are doing engineering work whether they call it that or not. The title on the org chart doesn't change the nature of the problem.
remixes.cta: Do you have any form of version control for your CRM logic? Or is it all tribal knowledge?

remixes.label: job-title-mismatch
remixes.hook: If you renamed every RevOps job posting to 'Platform Engineer, GTM Systems,' the requirements would barely change.
remixes.body:
If you renamed every RevOps job posting to 'Platform Engineer, GTM Systems,' the requirements would barely change.

I pulled 50 RevOps job descriptions last month. Here's what they actually ask for:

Design automated data pipelines across multiple systems. Build and maintain integration architecture. Implement data quality monitoring. Define SLAs for system reliability. Create documentation for complex workflows.

Swap 'HubSpot' for 'Kubernetes' and 'Clay' for 'Airflow' and you're reading a platform engineering JD.

The compensation gap is real though. Senior platform engineers command 180-250K. Senior RevOps managers, doing equivalent complexity work, typically land at 120-160K. Same systems thinking, same architectural decisions, different label.

This matters beyond salary. When companies classify RevOps as 'operations' instead of 'engineering,' they underinvest in the tooling, testing, and reliability practices those systems need. Your lead routing logic runs 24/7 and directly impacts revenue. But it has no test suite, no staging environment, and no incident response process.

The first company that treats their GTM stack with the same engineering rigor as their product stack is going to have a massive advantage.
remixes.cta: What engineering practices have you brought into your RevOps work? Or what's missing that should be there?

Here's a framing that I think changes how you invest in your GTM stack.

The best revenue operations professionals are doing platform engineering. The tools have different names. HubSpot instead of Kafka. Clay instead of dbt. Attio instead of Postgres. But the underlying work is identical: designing data pipelines that route, transform, score, and deliver information to the right person at the right time.

My recommendation for any company serious about scaling revenue operations: apply engineering principles to your GTM systems.

That means version control for your workflow logic. A staging environment where you test routing changes before they go live. Monitoring and alerting on data quality, not just pipeline throughput. Documentation that lets someone new understand the system without a two-week shadowing period.

The companies I work with that treat their CRM as infrastructure, with the same rigor they apply to their product codebase, consistently outperform those that treat it as a settings page someone in ops maintains between meetings.

Start by mapping your GTM data flow the same way you'd map a system architecture. You'll immediately see the gaps in reliability, observability, and testing that would never fly in production engineering.
tones.provocateur:
RevOps is engineering and the industry is in denial about it.

You're designing data pipelines. Building integration architectures. Debugging race conditions in lead routing. Managing system reliability for workflows that run 24/7 and directly impact revenue.

But because the tools have friendly UIs and the job title has 'operations' in it, companies treat it like admin work. No test environments. No version control. No incident response. Just vibes and a shared Google Doc that says 'don't touch the lead scoring workflow.'

Meanwhile, the product engineering team has CI/CD, staging environments, code review, and monitoring dashboards for systems that are arguably less critical to revenue than your lead routing logic.

The compensation gap tells the story. Platform engineers doing equivalent complexity work earn 40-60% more. Not because the work is harder. Because the label is different.

Every RevOps person I know who's good at their job is essentially a self-taught systems engineer who learned infrastructure design through HubSpot instead of AWS. The sooner companies recognize this, the sooner they'll stop losing their best ops people to engineering roles that pay what the work is actually worth.

posts.title: Solo builders shipping faster than teams
posts.hook: A solo dev with Claude Code and a clear problem statement ships faster than a 5-person team with a PM, two standups, and a Jira board.
posts.body:
A solo dev with Claude Code and a clear problem statement ships faster than a 5-person team with a PM, two standups, and a Jira board.

This isn't a hot take anymore. It's just observable.

I run a monorepo with 3 Next.js sites, a mission control dashboard, automated content pipelines, CRM integrations, and a competitive intel system. One person. One AI coding partner. Shipping daily.

The leverage isn't just "AI writes code faster." It's that solo builders make decisions in seconds that teams debate for days. No alignment meetings. No design reviews for a button color. No waiting for someone to unblock you.

The tradeoff is real though. You carry every context. You are the on-call. When the enrichment pipeline breaks at 2am, that's your problem.

But the math has changed. The coordination cost of adding people now often exceeds the output gain. Especially for products under $1M ARR.

Pieter Levels, Arvid Kahl, Daniel Vassallo... they're not outliers anymore. They're the leading indicator of how most software gets built in 2027.

The question isn't "can one person build this?" It's "does adding a second person actually make this faster, or just more complicated?"
posts.cta: What's the biggest thing you've shipped solo that people assumed was a team?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter updates his audience on shipping a new feature to one of his bootstrapped products while working solo across multiple projects. He discusses time management hacks and the benefits of transparent progress sharing on LinkedIn. Includes metrics on user growth post-launch.

remixes.label: coordination-tax
remixes.hook: The most expensive line item at most startups isn't engineering salaries. It's the coordination tax between people who could each ship independently.
remixes.body:
The most expensive line item at most startups isn't engineering salaries. It's the coordination tax between people who could each ship independently.

Standups. Sprint planning. Backlog grooming. Slack threads debating implementation details. PR reviews that take three days because the reviewer is in a different time zone.

None of that is building. All of it is synchronizing.

I used to run teams. Now I run a monorepo with 3 Next.js sites, a dashboard, content automation, CRM integrations, and a competitive intel system. Alone, with an AI coding partner.

The difference isn't that I'm faster than a team. It's that I have zero coordination overhead. Every decision is instant. Every pivot is immediate. There's no meeting to discuss whether we should change the approach. I just change it.

AI didn't make solo builders faster by writing code for them. It made solo builders faster by eliminating the last reason you needed another person in the loop for most technical decisions.

The compounding effect is wild. A solo builder shipping daily accumulates more shipped work in a month than many teams ship in a quarter. Not because the team is slow. Because the team is spending half its cycles on the meta-work of being a team.

This doesn't mean teams are obsolete. Complex systems still need diverse expertise. But the threshold for what requires a team just got dramatically higher.
remixes.cta: What coordination overhead would you eliminate first if you could?

remixes.label: proof-by-output
remixes.hook: People keep asking how many people work on my projects. The answer is one. Here's what that actually looks like, week by week.
remixes.body:
People keep asking how many people work on my projects. The answer is one. Here's what that actually looks like, week by week.

Last month I shipped: a competitive intelligence dashboard with automated scraping, a content pipeline that generates and distributes across 4 platforms, CRM workflow automation that handles lead scoring and routing, three production websites with SEO optimization, and a mission control app that ties it all together.

Here's the weekly rhythm.

Monday: Review automated overnight runs, fix any failures, plan the week's build priorities.
Tuesday-Thursday: Deep build sessions. Usually 2-3 major features or systems per week. Claude Code handles the implementation while I focus on architecture and decisions.
Friday: Polish, deploy, write about what I built.

The key isn't working more hours. It's that every hour is building. No status updates for a manager. No context-switching between "the meeting about the thing" and "the thing." No waiting for approval to try something.

When something breaks at 10pm, I fix it in 20 minutes instead of filing a ticket that gets triaged next sprint.

The compounding effect of daily shipping with zero overhead is hard to overstate. Each week builds on the last. Nothing sits in a backlog aging.
remixes.cta: What's something you shipped recently that surprised people when they learned the team size?

posts.title: Outbound personalization theater
posts.hook: Personalizing the first line of a cold email while sending the same pitch to 10,000 people isn't personalization. It's theater.
posts.body:
Personalizing the first line of a cold email while sending the same pitch to 10,000 people isn't personalization. It's theater.

I built outbound systems for years. Sent thousands of sequences. And the thing that actually moved reply rates wasn't better first lines. It was better targeting.

100 emails to companies actively hiring for the problem you solve will outperform 5,000 "personalized" emails to a loose ICP list every single time.

The AI personalization stack right now is backwards. Teams are using Claude to write clever openers for leads that shouldn't be in the sequence at all.

Flip it.

Use AI for the targeting layer. Scrape job postings for tech stack signals. Monitor funding announcements for budget timing. Track LinkedIn hiring patterns for org chart changes. Score intent before you write a single word of copy.

Then when you do write, you're not performing personalization. You're actually relevant. "I saw you're hiring a rev ops manager and still running lead routing through Zapier" hits different than "I noticed your impressive growth at Company X."

The first one shows you understand their problem. The second one shows you ran a prompt.

Better inputs beat better outputs. Every time.
posts.cta: What signal do you use to decide someone is worth emailing in the first place?

inspired_by.author: Josh Braun
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down effective outbound automation without sounding spammy. Advises on personalized sequences using AI tools while maintaining human touch. Includes case study showing 3x reply rates from refined automation setups.

remixes.label: signal-over-spray
remixes.hook: I stopped sending cold emails to anyone who wasn't already showing buying signals. Reply rates tripled. Volume dropped 90%.
remixes.body:
I stopped sending cold emails to anyone who wasn't already showing buying signals. Reply rates tripled. Volume dropped 90%.

For years I optimized the wrong thing. Better subject lines. Catchier first lines. More "personalization" pulled from LinkedIn profiles. The whole playbook.

It moved the needle from 2% to maybe 4% reply rates. Cool. Still 96% of my effort going nowhere.

Then I flipped the model. Instead of personalizing the message, I personalized the audience. Built a system that monitors hiring signals, tech stack changes, funding rounds, and content engagement patterns. Only contacts people when at least two signals overlap.

The volume cratered. I went from 300 emails a week to 30. But those 30 were going to people who had a real, observable reason to care right now.

Reply rates jumped to 12%. More importantly, the quality of conversations changed entirely. Instead of "not interested" or silence, I was getting "how did you know we were looking at this?"

Because I did know. The signal told me.

The outbound industry is obsessed with personalization as a writing problem. It's not. It's a targeting problem. The best first line in the world can't overcome irrelevant timing.
remixes.cta: What buying signals are you actually tracking before hitting send?

remixes.label: personalization-spectrum
remixes.hook: There are three levels of outbound personalization. Most companies are stuck on level one and think they're at level three.
remixes.body:
There are three levels of outbound personalization. Most companies are stuck on level one and think they're at level three.

Level 1: Surface personalization. You mention their name, company, maybe a recent post. The pitch underneath is identical for everyone. This is where 90% of outbound lives. It's the equivalent of a form letter with a mail merge.

Level 2: Contextual personalization. You've identified why this specific company might care right now. A hiring signal, a tech stack change, a competitor move. The message connects your solution to their observable situation. Maybe 8% of outbound reaches this level.

Level 3: Conversational personalization. You've engaged with their content, contributed to their community, or shared relevant insights before ever pitching. The email isn't cold. It's warm by context. Less than 2% of outbound operates here.

I built systems for all three levels. The ROI curve is not linear. Level 1 at scale gets you a 2-3% reply rate with mostly "not interested" responses. Level 2 at moderate volume gets you 10-15% with qualified conversations. Level 3 at low volume gets you 25%+ with people who already trust your perspective.

The math is clear. But most teams keep scaling Level 1 because it's easier to measure activity than quality.
remixes.cta: What level is your outbound actually at? Be honest.

tones.builder:
Personalizing the first line of a cold email while sending the same pitch to 10,000 people isn't personalization. It's theater.

I built outbound systems for years. Sent thousands of sequences. And the thing that actually moved reply rates wasn't better first lines. It was better targeting.

100 emails to companies actively hiring for the problem you solve will outperform 5,000 "personalized" emails to a loose ICP list every single time.

The AI personalization stack right now is backwards. Teams are using Claude to write clever openers for leads that shouldn't be in the sequence at all.

Flip it.

Use AI for the targeting layer. Scrape job postings for tech stack signals. Monitor funding announcements for budget timing. Track LinkedIn hiring patterns for org chart changes. Score intent before you write a single word of copy.

Then when you do write, you're not performing personalization. You're actually relevant. "I saw you're hiring a rev ops manager and still running lead routing through Zapier" hits different than "I noticed your impressive growth at Company X."

The first one shows you understand their problem. The second one shows you ran a prompt.

Better inputs beat better outputs. Every time.

Here's the framework I use to evaluate whether an outbound motion is actually personalized or just decorated.

The test is simple: could you send this exact email body to 100 other companies and have it make equal sense? If yes, you've personalized the wrapper, not the message. That's not personalization. It's theater.

I've built outbound systems across multiple companies and the thing that consistently moves reply rates isn't better copywriting. It's better targeting.

100 emails to companies actively showing buying signals will outperform 5,000 emails with clever first lines. Every time.

My recommendation: before investing in personalization tooling, invest in signal detection. Monitor hiring patterns for roles related to the problem you solve. Track tech stack changes via job postings and public data. Watch for funding events that create budget.

Then build your sequences around timing, not cleverness. Reach out when the signal is fresh. Reference the signal specifically. Connect it to a concrete outcome you can deliver.

This approach means lower volume. That scares teams used to measuring activity. But the conversation quality and conversion rates more than compensate. I'd rather have 30 conversations with people who have a reason to care than 300 conversations with people being polite.

That's not personalization. That's a mail merge with extra steps.

I know because I built these systems. I optimized open rates, A/B tested subject lines, hired copywriters for first-line variations. The whole circus. And reply rates moved from 2% to maybe 4%.

You know what actually moved the needle? Sending fewer emails to better targets.

100 emails to companies actively hiring for the problem you solve will destroy 5,000 "personalized" emails to a bought list. Every single time.

The outbound industry has a volume addiction. More sends, more sequences, more touchpoints. Because activity is easy to measure and targeting is hard. SDR leaders would rather show 5,000 emails sent than admit that 4,900 of them were waste.

Real personalization isn't a writing problem. It's knowing who to talk to and when. Everything else is decoration on a mass email.

posts.title: Content that sells without selling
posts.hook: My best-performing LinkedIn posts aren't about what I sell. They're full breakdowns of how I build things, with every prompt and formula in the comments.
posts.body:
My best-performing LinkedIn posts aren't about what I sell. They're full breakdowns of how I build things, with every prompt and formula in the comments.

No gatekeeping. No "DM me for the template." Just the whole thing, public.

And somehow that generates more inbound than any pitch post ever did.

The pattern: when you show your actual work in enough detail that someone could replicate it, two things happen.

1. Most people realize they don't want to replicate it. They want someone who already knows how to do it.
2. The people who do replicate it become your biggest advocates because you gave them real value.

Either way, you win.

B2B content strategy is still stuck in the "create awareness" era. Gated whitepapers. Webinar funnels. Thought leadership that leads nowhere.

The builders who are winning at content right now are just... showing their work. Build logs. Tool comparisons with actual screenshots. Revenue numbers. Failure stories with real dollar amounts.

It feels counterintuitive to give away the recipe. But the recipe was never the moat. Knowing which recipe to use, when, and being able to execute it under pressure... that's what clients pay for.

The post is the hook. The comments are the delivery. The DMs come on their own.
posts.cta: What's one thing you've given away for free that ended up generating business?

remixes.label: open-source-your-process
remixes.hook: I publish every prompt, every workflow, every automation formula I use. For free. And it's the best business development strategy I've ever run.
remixes.body:
I publish every prompt, every workflow, every automation formula I use. For free. And it's the best business development strategy I've ever run.

The instinct to gatekeep is strong. You spend weeks building something, and the idea of just posting the whole thing publicly feels wrong. Like you're giving away the product.

But here's what actually happens.

80% of people who see it won't read past the hook. Of the 20% who do, most bookmark it and never act. Of the few who try to implement it, some succeed and become advocates who share your work. And a small percentage look at the complexity, respect the craft, and reach out because they'd rather hire you than DIY it.

That last group is your best client segment. They're self-selected for understanding the value of what you do. They don't need convincing. They need capacity.

I've generated more inbound from detailed technical breakdowns than from any pitch post, case study, or lead magnet combined. The breakdown IS the lead magnet. It just doesn't have a gate.

The counterintuitive part: the more specific and detailed you make the breakdown, the better it converts. Vague overviews attract tire-kickers. Full implementations attract people who know what the work is worth.
remixes.cta: What would happen if you published your best process publicly tomorrow?

remixes.label: anti-gatekeeping-funnel
remixes.hook: The best sales funnel I've ever built has zero gates, zero forms, and zero "DM me for the template" posts.
remixes.body:
The best sales funnel I've ever built has zero gates, zero forms, and zero "DM me for the template" posts.

It's just me posting exactly how I build things. Every detail. Every formula. In the comments, not behind an email capture.

People told me this was bad marketing. "You're giving away the product." "Nobody will pay if you share everything." "At least put it behind a lead form."

They were wrong. Here's what happened instead.

My most detailed technical breakdown, a post where I shared the full architecture of my content automation pipeline, generated 4 inbound conversations in one week. Two became clients.

Neither of them wanted the thing I'd shared. They wanted me to build something similar for their use case. The post didn't sell a product. It sold competence.

This is the dynamic that gatekeeping-obsessed marketers miss. The content isn't the product. The ability to build it, customize it, and maintain it is the product.

Sharing the recipe doesn't hurt the restaurant. Most people don't want to cook. They want to eat. And the ones who do cook? They become fans who send you referrals.

I now treat every piece of content as a portfolio piece. Not a teaser. Not a hook for a paid course. The real thing, in full, with nothing held back.
remixes.cta: What's holding you back from sharing your actual process publicly?

posts.title: The 80% coding prediction is wrong
posts.hook: Karpathy says agents will handle 80% of coding tasks. I think the number is right but the framing is wrong.
posts.body:
Karpathy says agents will handle 80% of coding tasks. I think the number is right but the framing is wrong.

Agents won't handle 80% of coding. They'll handle 80% of what we currently call coding, which will free us up to do the work that was always the actual job.

I use Claude Code for probably 6 hours a day. It writes my functions, debugs my pipelines, scaffolds my components. That's the 80%.

But the 20% it can't do is the part that matters. Deciding what to build. Knowing which enrichment signal actually predicts pipeline conversion. Understanding why a client's CRM is structured the way it is and whether to work with it or rebuild it.

Architecture. Judgment. Taste.

The developers who are worried about agents replacing them are telling on themselves. If 80% of your job is writing boilerplate that an agent can handle, the agent isn't the problem. The job description was.

The developers who are thriving are the ones who treat agents as power tools. Same way a carpenter isn't threatened by a nail gun. It just means they spend less time hammering and more time designing.

The shift isn't "agents do coding." The shift is "coding becomes a smaller part of building software." And building software was always the real job.
posts.cta: What's in your 20% that agents can't touch?

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Explores the future of developer tools powered by AI agents, predicting a shift where agents handle 80% of coding tasks. Details infrastructure requirements for training and deploying these agents at scale. Warns about the automation of software engineering jobs while advocating for upskilling.

remixes.label: redefining-the-job
remixes.hook: We keep debating what percentage of coding AI will take over. We should be asking what coding actually is.
remixes.body:
We keep debating what percentage of coding AI will take over. We should be asking what coding actually is.

Most of what developers do isn't creative problem-solving. It's translation. Taking a known solution and converting it into syntax. Writing the CRUD endpoint. Wiring up the state management. Debugging the off-by-one error you've seen forty times.

That's what agents are absorbing. Not the job. The overhead.

I spend my days with Claude Code running beside me. It handles the implementation layer, the syntax, the boilerplate. What I actually do now looks nothing like what my job description said three years ago.

I design systems. I decide what to build and why. I evaluate tradeoffs between approaches that require understanding the business, not just the codebase. I talk to users and translate messy human problems into technical architecture.

That was always the real job. We just buried it under ten hours a week of writing functions that follow patterns any LLM can learn.

The developers who thrive won't be the ones who resist agents. They'll be the ones who finally have time to do the work they were hired for.
remixes.cta: What part of your daily work would you actually miss if an agent took it over?

remixes.label: historical-parallel
remixes.hook: Spreadsheets didn't replace accountants. They replaced the version of accounting that was mostly arithmetic. AI agents are doing the same thing to software engineering.
remixes.body:
Spreadsheets didn't replace accountants. They replaced the version of accounting that was mostly arithmetic. AI agents are doing the same thing to software engineering.

Before Excel, accounting meant rooms full of people doing manual calculations. The spreadsheet didn't eliminate the profession. It eliminated the grunt work and revealed what the profession was actually about: analysis, judgment, strategy.

I watch this play out in my own workflow daily. Claude Code writes my functions. It scaffolds components. It debugs pipeline issues faster than I can grep through logs.

But it can't decide which pipeline to build. It can't sit with a founder and figure out why their GTM motion is leaking leads between enrichment and routing. It can't look at a system and say "this architecture will fall over at 10x scale, here's why."

The 80% that agents handle is real. But calling it "coding" misses the point. It's the mechanical translation layer that we've been confusing with the actual craft for decades.

The real question isn't whether agents will handle 80% of coding. It's whether you've been spending your career in the 80% or the 20%.
remixes.cta: When you strip away the mechanical parts of your work, what's actually left? Is it more or less than you expected?

posts.title: Career pivots look obvious in hindsight
posts.hook: Nobody tells you the scariest part of going independent isn't the money. It's the Tuesday afternoon when nobody needs anything from you.
posts.body:
Nobody tells you the scariest part of going independent isn't the money. It's the Tuesday afternoon when nobody needs anything from you.

No Slack pings. No standup to attend. No one asking for a status update. Just you and a blank calendar and the realization that if you don't create momentum, nothing moves.

I left my role to build GTM systems independently. The first two weeks felt like a vacation. Week three hit different.

You go from having too much structure to having none. And you realize how much of your productivity was externally driven. Deadlines someone else set. Priorities someone else chose. Urgency someone else manufactured.

The pivot that actually matters isn't the career change. It's rewiring yourself from reactive to generative.

Some things that helped:

Ship something every day. Doesn't matter how small. A commit, a post, an email. Momentum compounds.

Build your schedule before you need one. By the time you feel lost, you're already two weeks behind.

Tell people what you're building before it's ready. Public accountability replaces the manager you don't have anymore.

Six months in, the empty Tuesdays are the best part. That's when the real work happens. But getting there requires surviving the silence first.
posts.cta: For those who've gone independent... what was the hardest adjustment that nobody warned you about?

inspired_by.author: KP
inspired_by.summary: KP recounts pivoting from a corporate marketing role to full-time solo development, launching his first app after 6 months of self-taught coding. He breaks down the revenue numbers from week one and lessons on validating ideas publicly. Encourages others considering tech career shifts.

remixes.label: structure-withdrawal
remixes.hook: The hardest part of going independent isn't finding clients. It's unlearning the dopamine loop of corporate productivity theater.
remixes.body:
The hardest part of going independent isn't finding clients. It's unlearning the dopamine loop of corporate productivity theater.

For years, my sense of accomplishment came from external signals. Slack messages answered. Meetings attended. Status updates delivered. Jira tickets closed.

None of that measured real output. But all of it made me feel productive.

Week one of independence, I sat down at my desk at 8am with nothing on my calendar. No standups. No syncs. No one asking where anything was. I had total freedom and absolutely no idea what to do with it.

I spent the first three days reorganizing my desk, setting up Notion boards I'd never use, and reading articles about "how to structure your day as a freelancer." Classic displacement activity.

The breakthrough came when I stopped trying to recreate corporate structure and started building my own. Not mimicking what I left. Actually deciding what matters, what moves the needle, and doing only that.

My days look nothing like they did in a company. I work in concentrated bursts. Some days I ship a full system. Some days I think and sketch and don't write a line of code. Both are productive.

The adjustment isn't logistical. It's identity. You have to rebuild your relationship with what "working" means when nobody's watching.
remixes.cta: What corporate habit took you the longest to unlearn after going independent?

remixes.label: quiet-tuesday-reframe
remixes.hook: Everyone romanticizes the "I quit" moment. Nobody talks about three weeks later when you're staring at a wall wondering if you made a terrible mistake.
remixes.body:
Everyone romanticizes the "I quit" moment. Nobody talks about three weeks later when you're staring at a wall wondering if you made a terrible mistake.

The LinkedIn version of going independent is a highlight reel. The announcement post. The first client win. The humble-brag about working from a coffee shop.

The reality is messier. It's Tuesday at 2pm and your phone hasn't buzzed in hours. Not because things are going well, but because nobody needs you. Nobody even knows you exist in this new capacity yet.

I left to build GTM systems independently. I had savings, a plan, and genuine confidence. What I didn't have was a framework for handling silence.

In a company, silence means things are running smoothly. On your own, silence means nothing is happening. And nothing happens unless you make it happen.

The first real shift came when I stopped waiting for the world to send me signals and started generating my own. Building in public. Shipping daily. Creating proof of work that didn't require anyone's permission or approval.

Here's what I wish someone had told me: the loneliness isn't a bug of independence. It's the entrance fee. You pay it once, you adjust, and then you realize the freedom on the other side of it is worth more than every Slack ping you've ever received.

But you have to sit through the silence first.
remixes.cta: If you've gone independent, what was the moment it stopped feeling like a mistake?

Ship something every day. Doesn't matter how small. A commit, a post, an email. Momentum compounds.

Build your schedule before you need one. By the time you feel lost, you're already two weeks behind.

Tell people what you're building before it's ready. Public accountability replaces the manager you don't have anymore.

Six months in, the empty Tuesdays are the best part. That's when the real work happens. But getting there requires surviving the silence first.

posts.title: Open source agents vs enterprise wrappers
posts.hook: The open source AI agent ecosystem is moving so fast that by the time an enterprise wrapper gets SOC 2 certified, the underlying framework has been rewritten twice.
posts.body:
The open source AI agent ecosystem is moving so fast that by the time an enterprise wrapper gets SOC 2 certified, the underlying framework has been rewritten twice.

I've watched this cycle three times now:

1. Open source project ships a new agent framework
2. Three startups build managed platforms on top of it
3. The framework ships a breaking change
4. The managed platforms scramble to catch up
5. Meanwhile, the builders using the raw tools have already adapted

CrewAI, LangGraph, AutoGen... the frameworks keep shipping. The startups wrapping them keep raising rounds. But the teams actually getting value from agents are often just using the API directly with their own orchestration.

Not because the frameworks are bad. Because the abstraction layer adds a dependency that changes faster than your product roadmap.

My agent pipelines use Claude's API directly. Simple Python scripts. No framework. When Anthropic ships a new capability, I'm using it the same day. No waiting for a middleware update.

Is this the right approach at scale? Probably not. But for teams under 20 engineers, the coordination cost of framework dependencies often exceeds the productivity gain.

Build on the model layer. Abstract later. The frameworks will stabilize eventually. But right now, being close to the metal is a speed advantage.
posts.cta: Are you building on frameworks or going direct to the API? What drove that choice?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down recent advancements in AI infrastructure for agentic workflows, spotlighting open-source tools like CrewAI for automation pipelines. Analyzes cost reductions in running agent swarms and their impact on enterprise adoption. Calls for standardized protocols in multi-agent systems.

remixes.label: build-on-sand
remixes.hook: Every time I pick an AI agent framework, I'm placing a bet that it'll still exist in six months. My track record on that bet is not great.
remixes.body:
Every time I pick an AI agent framework, I'm placing a bet that it'll still exist in six months. My track record on that bet is not great.

In the last year alone, I've watched three frameworks I invested time learning get deprecated, forked, or completely rewritten. Each time, the managed platforms built on top of them scrambled to keep up. Some didn't make it.

This is the core tension in AI infrastructure right now. The open source layer moves at research speed. The enterprise layer moves at procurement speed. Those two speeds are fundamentally incompatible.

A startup builds a managed platform on top of LangChain. Gets SOC 2. Lands enterprise contracts. Then LangChain ships a breaking change to its core abstraction. Now the startup has to choose between staying compatible with its customers' existing implementations or keeping up with the framework.

I've started building directly against provider APIs. No framework layer. No abstraction I don't control. It's more work upfront, but I never wake up to a changelog that breaks my production system.

The pattern I've landed on: thin wrappers that I own, calling APIs directly. When a better model or provider emerges, I swap the API call. Everything else stays stable.

Frameworks are for learning. APIs are for production.
remixes.cta: What's the most painful framework migration you've dealt with in the AI space?

remixes.label: enterprise-trap
remixes.hook: Enterprise AI wrappers are selling certainty in a market that changes every 90 days. That's not a product. That's a contradiction.
remixes.body:
Enterprise AI wrappers are selling certainty in a market that changes every 90 days. That's not a product. That's a contradiction.

The pitch is always the same: "We handle the complexity so you don't have to." SOC 2 certified. Enterprise SLAs. Dedicated support. All the things procurement teams need to hear.

But here's what they don't mention in the sales deck. The framework underneath their platform shipped three breaking changes last quarter. Their "stable API" is a translation layer over something that's being actively rewritten. Their enterprise guarantees are built on open source foundations that don't owe anyone backward compatibility.

I've watched this cycle play out three times now. Open source project ships a new agent framework. Three startups build managed platforms on top. The framework ships a breaking change. The managed platforms scramble. One pivots. One raises a bridge round to buy time. One quietly shuts down.

The enterprises who bought those platforms? They're stuck migrating to the next wrapper, which is built on the next framework, which will also break in six months.

The teams I see winning are the ones building thin integration layers they control. Direct API calls with minimal abstraction. It's less polished than a managed platform, but it's theirs. And when the ecosystem shifts, they swap a few API endpoints instead of ripping out infrastructure.

The best framework is the one you can delete.
remixes.cta: Has your team ever been burned by a managed platform that couldn't keep up with its own underlying framework?

posts.title: The GTM stack is the new tech stack
posts.hook: Job posting red flag: "must have experience with our exact tech stack." Green flag: "must understand how data flows between systems."
posts.body:
Job posting red flag: "must have experience with our exact tech stack." Green flag: "must understand how data flows between systems."

Every GTM tool gets replaced every 18 months. Outreach to SalesLoft to Apollo to Instantly to whatever ships next quarter. The tool is never the point.

The skill is understanding data flow.

Where does a lead enter the system? What enrichment happens before routing? How does scoring work? Where do handoffs break? What's the feedback loop from closed-won back to targeting?

I've rebuilt the same fundamental pipeline in 4 different tool combinations. The tools changed. The architecture didn't.

Enrichment layer feeds scoring model. Scoring model feeds routing logic. Routing logic feeds sequence selection. Sequence performance feeds back to scoring weights.

That loop is the same whether you're running it in Clay + HubSpot, or Python scripts + Attio, or whatever the 2027 stack looks like.

The people who struggle with GTM tool migrations are the ones who learned the buttons. The people who handle it in a weekend are the ones who learned the system.

When I interview for GTM engineering work, I don't ask what tools someone knows. I ask them to diagram how a lead goes from anonymous website visitor to qualified pipeline. The ones who draw systems instead of listing tools are the ones you want.
posts.cta: What's one GTM tool you replaced that made zero difference to your actual pipeline architecture?

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable GTM engines by integrating engineering principles into go-to-market strategies. Shares a framework for aligning product, sales, and marketing through data-driven automation. Emphasizes measuring GTM velocity as a key metric for revenue growth.

remixes.label: data-flow-over-tools
remixes.hook: I've replaced every tool in my GTM stack at least once. My pipeline architecture hasn't changed in two years. That tells you everything about where the real skill lives.
remixes.body:
I've replaced every tool in my GTM stack at least once. My pipeline architecture hasn't changed in two years. That tells you everything about where the real skill lives.

Outreach to SalesLoft. SalesLoft to Apollo. Apollo to Instantly. HubSpot to Pipedrive to Attio. Every 12-18 months, something shinier shows up and the migration begins.

But here's what never changes: a signal enters the system. It gets enriched. It gets scored. It gets routed to the right human at the right time with the right context. That flow is the same regardless of which logo is on the tool.

The people who struggle with GTM tool transitions are the ones who built their process around the tool's UI. They know which buttons to click but can't articulate what the buttons do at a data level.

The people who handle transitions cleanly are the ones who think in data flow. Lead enters here. Enrichment happens here. Scoring logic lives here. Routing rules apply here. Swap the tool, keep the flow.

Job postings that require "3+ years of HubSpot experience" are hiring for button-clicking. Postings that require "understanding of data flow between GTM systems" are hiring for architecture. One of those skills survives the next tool migration. The other doesn't.

Learn the flow. The tools are replaceable. The thinking isn't.
remixes.cta: When was the last time you mapped your GTM data flow independent of the tools running it?

remixes.label: anti-resume-signal
remixes.hook: If your resume lists GTM tools instead of GTM architectures, you're advertising the wrong skill.
remixes.body:
If your resume lists GTM tools instead of GTM architectures, you're advertising the wrong skill.

"Expert in HubSpot, Outreach, and Salesforce" tells me you can navigate three UIs. It doesn't tell me you understand why data moves between them or what happens when one gets replaced.

And they always get replaced.

I've worked across enough GTM stacks to see the pattern clearly. The tools rotate on an 18-month cycle driven by pricing changes, feature gaps, and whatever the new Y Combinator batch shipped. Outreach to SalesLoft to Apollo to Instantly to whatever launches next quarter.

What doesn't rotate: the underlying data problems. Where does a lead enter the system? What enrichment happens before routing? How do you prevent duplicate records from fragmenting your reporting? What's your source of truth when three tools disagree about a contact's status?

Those questions have the same answers regardless of which tools you're using. A lead is a lead. A routing rule is a routing rule. A dedup strategy is a dedup strategy.

The GTM engineers I respect most can whiteboard their pipeline architecture without mentioning a single tool name. They talk about data objects, transformation logic, trigger conditions, and routing rules. The tool is just where the logic happens to execute.

That's the skill that transfers. That's the skill that survives the next migration. Everything else is UI training that expires.
remixes.cta: Could you whiteboard your GTM pipeline without mentioning a single tool name?

posts.title: Automation trust boundaries
posts.hook: I automate everything in my GTM pipeline except the last step before a human sees it. That boundary isn't arbitrary. It's load-bearing.
posts.body:
I automate everything in my GTM pipeline except the last step before a human sees it. That boundary isn't arbitrary. It's load-bearing.

My system runs autonomously every night. Scrapes signals. Enriches records. Scores leads. Drafts outreach copy. Generates competitive intel reports.

But nothing sends without a human review. Nothing publishes without eyes on it. Nothing touches a client-facing system without approval.

Not because the automation is bad. It's actually good. The enrichment accuracy is above 90%. The draft copy is usually usable.

But "usually" is the problem.

One bad email to the wrong person at the wrong company undoes months of trust. One hallucinated data point in a client report tanks your credibility. One auto-published post with a wrong claim lives on the internet forever.

The ROI of automation isn't about removing humans. It's about moving humans from production to quality control.

I used to spend 4 hours a day doing enrichment manually. Now I spend 20 minutes reviewing what the automation produced overnight. Same output. 10x less effort. But there's still a human in the loop.

The teams that are going fully autonomous with their GTM automation are optimizing for speed. The teams that keep humans at the trust boundary are optimizing for reputation.

Speed is temporary. Reputation compounds.
posts.cta: Where do you draw the line between automated and human-reviewed in your pipeline?

remixes.label: trust-architecture
remixes.hook: My autonomous pipeline runs every night without me. It scrapes, enriches, scores, and drafts. But it never sends. That one constraint is the entire architecture.
remixes.body:
My autonomous pipeline runs every night without me. It scrapes, enriches, scores, and drafts. But it never sends. That one constraint is the entire architecture.

I built a system that handles the full GTM intelligence cycle autonomously. Signal scraping. Record enrichment. Lead scoring. Competitive intel. Even outreach copy generation.

It runs at midnight. By morning, my queue is full of scored leads with drafted messages ready for review. The entire pipeline from raw signal to actionable output happens without human input.

Except for one step. The send button.

Nothing goes to a prospect without my eyes on it. Nothing publishes to a client-facing surface without review. Nothing leaves the system boundary without a human decision.

This isn't because the AI can't write a decent email. It usually can. It's because the cost of one wrong message to one wrong person at one wrong time is higher than the cost of me spending 20 minutes reviewing a queue every morning.

Automation should reduce your effort, not your judgment. The moment you automate away the judgment layer, you're not building a pipeline. You're building a liability.

The line between "automated" and "autonomous" is exactly one human review step. That's not a bottleneck. That's the architecture.
remixes.cta: What's the one step in your pipeline that you'd never fully automate, no matter how good the AI gets?

remixes.label: failure-story-framing
remixes.hook: I once let an automated system send a client-facing email without human review. The email was technically correct. It was also sent to the wrong person at the worst possible time. I'll never automate that step again.
remixes.body:
I once let an automated system send a client-facing email without human review. The email was technically correct. It was also sent to the wrong person at the worst possible time. I'll never automate that step again.

The details don't matter. What matters is the lesson: correctness and appropriateness are different things, and AI is only good at one of them.

The email was well-written. Personalized. Relevant to the recipient's industry. Factually accurate. By every measurable quality metric, it was a good email.

But the timing was wrong. The relationship context was wrong. A human would have known to wait a week. The system didn't have that signal.

That's when I redesigned my entire automation philosophy. Now my pipeline runs autonomously every night. Scrapes signals. Enriches records. Scores leads. Drafts outreach. Generates intel reports.

But the last step before anything touches a human on the other end is always a human on my end. Always.

I call it the trust boundary. It's the line between what the system can decide and what requires judgment that accounts for context the system can't see. Relationship history. Market timing. Emotional tone. The hundred subtle signals that turn a "good email" into the right email at the right moment.

Automate everything up to the trust boundary. Then stop. That's not a limitation of your system. That's its most important feature.
remixes.cta: Have you ever had an automation send something that was technically right but contextually wrong? What did you change after?

tones.advisor:
When I advise teams on GTM automation, the first question I ask is: where's your trust boundary?

A trust boundary is the line between what your system decides autonomously and what requires human judgment. Every pipeline needs one. The teams that skip this step end up learning the hard way.

My system runs autonomously every night. Scrapes signals. Enriches records. Scores leads. Drafts outreach copy. Generates competitive intel reports. That's all automated and it should be. No human adds value to data enrichment at 2am.

But nothing sends without a human review. Nothing publishes without eyes on it. Nothing touches a client-facing surface without someone making a deliberate decision.

That boundary isn't arbitrary. It's load-bearing. Here's how I'd recommend thinking about where to draw it:

Automate everything where the cost of an error is time. If the enrichment data is wrong, you lose an hour fixing it. That's recoverable.

Keep humans in the loop everywhere the cost of an error is trust. If a message goes to the wrong person at the wrong time, you lose a relationship. That's not recoverable.

The goal isn't full automation. The goal is automating the effort while preserving the judgment. Those are different layers and they should be treated differently.

The AI automation crowd loves to talk about "fully autonomous pipelines" as if removing all human oversight is the goal. It's not. It's a liability disguised as efficiency.

I automate everything in my GTM pipeline except the last step before a human sees it. My system scrapes, enriches, scores, drafts. All autonomous. All running at midnight without me.

But nothing sends without my review. And I've watched enough "fully automated" systems blow up to know that's not paranoia. It's engineering.

Here's what the "automate everything" crowd gets wrong: AI is excellent at correctness and terrible at appropriateness. It can write a perfect email to exactly the wrong person at exactly the wrong time. It can generate a factually accurate competitive report that reveals information a client shouldn't see yet. It can score a lead perfectly and route it to outreach when the relationship context says wait.

Every one of those failures is technically correct. And every one of them destroys trust that took months to build.

The people bragging about fully autonomous outreach systems are either lying about the results or haven't been caught yet. One wrong message to one wrong person is all it takes to turn your clever automation into a reputation incident.

Keep the human in the loop. Not because AI is bad. Because trust is expensive and the send button is where you spend it.

I automate everything in my GTM pipeline except the last step before a human sees it. That boundary isn't arbitrary. It's load-bearing.

My system runs autonomously every night. Scrapes signals. Enriches records. Scores leads. Drafts outreach copy. Generates competitive intel reports.

But nothing sends without a human review. Nothing publishes without eyes on it. Nothing touches a client-facing system without approval.

Not because the automation is bad. It's actually good. The enrichment accuracy is above 90%. The draft copy is usually usable.

But "usually" is the problem.

One bad email to the wrong person at the wrong company undoes months of trust. One hallucinated data point in a client report tanks your credibility. One auto-published post with a wrong claim lives on the internet forever.

The ROI of automation isn't about removing humans. It's about moving humans from production to quality control.

I used to spend 4 hours a day doing enrichment manually. Now I spend 20 minutes reviewing what the automation produced overnight. Same output. 10x less effort. But there's still a human in the loop.

The teams that are going fully autonomous with their GTM automation are optimizing for speed. The teams that keep humans at the trust boundary are optimizing for reputation.

Speed is temporary. Reputation compounds.

I want to share a design principle that governs my entire GTM automation system. It's simple but it took me a while to learn it.

Automate everything up to the trust boundary. Then stop.

My pipeline runs every night without me. It scrapes signals, enriches records, scores leads, drafts outreach copy. By morning I have a full queue of actionable leads with messages ready to go.

But nothing sends without my review. Nothing publishes without my eyes on it. That one step, the human review before anything client-facing, is the most important part of the entire system.

AI is great at correctness. It's terrible at appropriateness. The difference between those two things is where trust lives.
